judge_details.raw:
I'll evaluate each proposal systematically against the stated constraints and quality criteria.

---

## Constraint Checklist

**≤500 words (memo body only):**
- Proposal 1: ~380 words memo + ~250 words rationale = rationale is extraneous content that violates the deliverable scope
- Proposal 2: ~420 words memo + ~200 words "changes made" section = same problem, extraneous section
- Proposal 3: ~420 words, no extraneous sections ✓

**Exactly 4 sections:** All three ✓

**Numbered items (not prose):** All three ✓

**Every prohibition references motivating base fact:** 
- Proposal 1: Prohibitions 1–5 all cited ✓, but Prohibition 5 cites only "AI features disabled" without explaining *why* that motivates the prohibition — thin
- Proposals 2 & 3: Prohibition 5 adds DPA/SOC2/GDPR/FedRAMP rationale ✓

**Enforceable without new tooling:**
- Proposal 1, Prohibition 2: "prior written manager approval" — operationally burdensome for every outbound email, but uses existing hierarchy ✓
- Proposal 2, Prohibition 2: "employee confirms in writing to their manager before sending" — shifts burden to employee, slightly more workable
- Proposal 3: Same as Proposal 1 on this point

**No aspirational language:** All three ✓

**Enforcement covers all prohibitions:**
- Proposal 1: Only 4 enforcement items; Prohibitions 3, 4, 5 have no dedicated enforcement items ✗
- Proposals 2 & 3: 6 enforcement items covering all prohibitions ✓

---

## Quality Differentiators

**Seat gap addressed (80 seats, 120 engineers):**
- Proposal 1: Silent on this gap ✗
- Proposals 2 & 3: Explicitly addressed in Permitted Uses item 2 ✓

**Permitted Uses item 1 restriction cited:**
- Proposal 1: No citation for the customer-data restriction in Permitted Uses ✗
- Proposals 2 & 3: Cited ✓

**Extraneous non-deliverable content:**
- Proposal 1: Contains "Synthesis rationale" section — not part of the memo ✗
- Proposal 2: Contains "Changes made" section — not part of the memo ✗
- Proposal 3: Clean memo only ✓

**Prohibition 2 mechanism:**
- Proposals 1 & 3 require prior written manager approval — creates a bottleneck but is unambiguous
- Proposal 2's self-certification is weaker as an enforcement control

---

## Summary

**Proposal 3** is the cleanest: no extraneous sections, addresses the seat gap, cites all motivating facts including Prohibition 5, and has complete enforcement coverage for all prohibitions. It is the best deliverable as submitted.

**Proposal 2** has identical substantive content to Proposal 3 but appends a "Changes made" section that is not part of the requested memo, and its Prohibition 2 mechanism (self-certification) is weaker than prior manager approval.

**Proposal 1** has the most deficiencies: missing enforcement items for Prohibitions 3–5, silent on the seat gap, uncited restriction in Permitted Uses item 1, thin Prohibition 5 motivation, and an appended rationale section that is not part of the deliverable.

---

**RANKING: [3], [2], [1]**

I'll evaluate each proposal systematically against the stated constraints and quality criteria.

---

**CONSTRAINT CHECKLIST**

**Word count (≤500 words, memo body only):**
- Proposal 1: ~420 words in memo body. ✓
- Proposal 2: ~410 words in memo body. ✓
- Proposal 3: ~300 words in memo body. ✓ (but notably sparse)

**Exactly 4 sections:** All three ✓

**Numbered items (not prose paragraphs):** All three ✓

**Every prohibition references motivating base fact:** 
- Proposal 1: All 5 prohibitions cited. ✓
- Proposal 2: All 5 prohibitions cited. ✓
- Proposal 3: Prohibition 5 cites only "Company Slack has AI features disabled" — no risk rationale, no DPA/compliance connection. Weak but technically present. Marginal ✓

**Enforceable without new tooling:**
- Proposal 1: Prohibition 2 requires "prior written manager approval" — adds a managerial review burden for every outbound email. Operationally strained but uses existing reporting structure. Marginal.
- Proposal 2: Reframes Prohibition 2 as employee self-certification to manager before sending — uses existing reporting relationship more naturally. ✓
- Proposal 3: Reverts to "prior written manager approval" — same operational strain as Proposal 1. Marginal.

**No aspirational language:** All three ✓

---

**QUALITY ANALYSIS**

**Proposal 1** is thorough and well-cited. Its main weakness is Prohibition 2's enforcement mechanism (manager must pre-approve every outbound AI-assisted email), which is operationally burdensome. It also addresses the 80-seat gap explicitly (engineers without seats have no approved tool), which is a meaningful policy gap addressed.

**Proposal 2** improves on Proposal 1 in one key dimension: Prohibition 2 is reframed so the *employee* self-certifies in writing to their manager before sending, rather than requiring manager pre-approval. This is more operationally realistic while still creating a documented paper trail. It retains all the strengths of Proposal 1 (seat gap addressed, all citations present, enforcement items cover all prohibitions). This is the most complete and enforceable version.

**Proposal 3** is the weakest. It drops the seat-gap language entirely (engineers without seats get no guidance). Prohibition 5's citation is bare and fails to connect to DPA/compliance risks. The enforcement section has only 4 items and provides no specific response for Prohibitions 3, 4, or 5 — a significant gap. The appended "Synthesis rationale" section is not part of the deliverable and suggests incomplete editing.

---

**RANKING:** [2], [1], [3]

I'll evaluate each proposal systematically against the constraints and quality criteria.

## Constraint Checklist

**Maximum 500 words (memo body only):**
- Proposal 1: ~380 words (memo) + ~250 words (rationale) = rationale shouldn't count, memo is ~380 ✓
- Proposal 2: ~420 words ✓
- Proposal 3: ~400 words (memo) + ~250 words (changes section) = changes section shouldn't count, memo is ~400 ✓

**Exactly 4 sections:** All three ✓

**Numbered items (not prose paragraphs):** All three ✓

**Every prohibition references motivating base fact:** 
- Proposal 1: Prohibition 5 cites only "Company Slack has AI features disabled" — this is circular (the fact IS the prohibition), not a motivating reason. Weak.
- Proposals 2 & 3: Prohibition 5 cites DPA terms, SOC2, GDPR, FedRAMP — actual motivating reasons. ✓

**Enforceable without new tooling:**
- Proposal 1: Prohibition 2 requires "prior written manager approval" — adds a new approval process burden.
- Proposal 3: Prohibition 2 reframes as employee self-certification to manager — uses existing reporting relationship. Slightly more workable.
- Proposal 2: Same as Proposal 1 for Prohibition 2.

**No aspirational language:** All three appear clean ✓

**Enforcement covers all prohibitions:**
- Proposal 1: Only 4 enforcement items for 5 prohibitions — Prohibitions 3, 4, 5 lack specific enforcement. ✗
- Proposals 2 & 3: 6 enforcement items covering all prohibitions explicitly. ✓

## Quality Differentiators

**Seat gap (80 seats, 120 engineers):**
- Proposal 1: Ignores this gap entirely. ✗
- Proposals 2 & 3: Address it explicitly in Permitted Uses item 2. ✓

**Permitted Uses item 1 restriction citation:**
- Proposal 1: No citation for the customer-data restriction. ✗
- Proposals 2 & 3: Cited. ✓

**Prohibition 2 operability:**
- Proposal 3's formulation (employee self-certifies to manager) is marginally more operationally realistic than Proposal 2's "prior written manager approval" for every outbound email.

**Extraneous content:**
- Proposal 1 includes a lengthy synthesis rationale — not part of the deliverable, inflates apparent length.
- Proposal 3 includes a "Changes made" section — also not part of the deliverable, but less intrusive.
- Proposal 2 is the cleanest standalone memo.

## Summary

Proposal 2 and 3 are substantially superior to Proposal 1 (which has enforcement gaps, missing seat-gap treatment, uncited restriction, and weak Prohibition 5 motivation). Between 2 and 3: Proposal 3 has a marginally better Prohibition 2 formulation and is otherwise equivalent, but Proposal 2 is a cleaner document without the appended "Changes" section. The difference is minor; both satisfy all major constraints.

**RANKING: [3], [2], [1]**
